analogy: Think of "Migration" through the chapter's own opening images: Unit II Unit II Unit II Unit II Unit II Chapter 2 HUMAN SETTLEMENTS Human Settlement means cluster of dwellings of any type or size where human beings live. For this purpose, people may erect houses and other structures and command some area or territory... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Migration is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that unit II Unit II Unit II Unit II Unit II Chapter 2 HUMAN SETTLEMENTS Human Settlement means cluster of dwellings of any type or size where human beings live.

The text explains that for this purpose, people may erect houses and other structures and command some area or territory as their economic support-base.

The text explains that thus, the process of settlement inherently involves grouping of people and apportioning of territory as their resource base.

The text explains that they range from a hamlet to metropolitan cities.

The text explains that with size, the economic character and social structure of settlements changes and so do its ecology and technology.

The text explains that settlements could be small and sparsely spaced; they may also be large and closely spaced.

The text explains that the sparsely located small settlements are called villages, specialising in agriculture or other primary activities.

The text explains that on the other hand, there are fewer but larger settlements which are termed as urban settlements specialising in secondary and tertiary activities.

The text explains that the basic differences between rural and urban settlements are as follows : • The rural settlements derive their life support or basic economic needs from land based primary economic activities, whereas, urban settlements, depend on processing of raw materials and manufacturing of finished goods on the one hand and a variety of services on the other. • Cities act as nodes of economic growth, provide goods and services not only to urban dwellers but also to the people of the rural settlements in their hinterlands in return for food and raw materials.

The text explains that this functional relationship between the urban and rural settlements takes place through transport and communication network. • Rural and urban settlements differ in terms of social relationship, attitude and outlook.

The text explains that rural people are less mobile and therefore, social relations among them are intimate.

The text explains that in urban areas, on the other hand, way of life is complex and fast, and social relations are formal.

The text explains that t T T T Types of ypes of ypes of ypes of ypes of R R R R Rur ur ur ur ural Settlement al Settlement al Settlement al Settlement al Settlement Types of the settlement are determined by the extent of the built-up area and inter-house

The text explains that 16 India : People and Economy Semi-Clustered Settlements Semi-clustered or fragmented settlements may result from tendency of clustering in a restricted area of dispersed settlement.

The text explains that more often such a pattern may also result from segregation or fragmentation of a large compact village.

The text explains that in this case, one or more sections of the village society choose or is forced to live a little away from the main cluster or village.

The text explains that in Rajasthan, scarcity of water has necessitated compact settlement for maximum utilisation of available water resources.

The text explains that extreme dispersion of settlement is often caused by extremely fragmented nature of the terrain and land resource base of habitable areas.

The text explains that thus, exchange of goods and services is performed sometimes directly and sometimes through a series of market towns and cities.

The text explains that thus, cities are connected directly as well as indirectly with the villages and also with each other. extending their domination either directly or through control over the princely states, they established their administrative centres, hill- towns as summer resorts, and added new civil, The census of India, 1991 defines urban settlements as “All places which have municipality, corporation, cantonment board or notified town area committee and have a minimum population of 5000 persons, at least 75 per cent of male workers are engaged in non-agriculture pursuits and a density of population of at least 400 persons per square kilometer are urban.

The text explains that b u t t h e g r o w t h r a t e o f urbanisation has slowed down during last two decades. *Source: Census of India, 2011 http.//www.censusindia.gov.in (Provisional) Functional Classification of Towns Apart from their role as central or nodal places, many towns and cities perform specialised services.

The text explains that mining towns These towns have developed in mineral rich areas such as Raniganj, Jharia, Digboi, Ankaleshwar, Singrauli, etc.

The text explains that smart Cities Mission The objective of the Smart Cities Mission is to promote cities that provide core infrastructure, a clean and sustainable environment and give a decent quality of life to its citizens.

The text explains that for example, making areas less vulnerable to disasters, using fewer resources and providing cheaper services.

The text explains that (a) Population density of 400 persons per sq km.

The text explains that (b) Presence of municipality, corporation, etc.

The text explains that (c) More than 75% of the population engaged in primary sector.

The text explains that (d) Population size of more than 5,000 persons.

The text explains that (iii) In which one of the following environments does one expect the presence of dispersed rural settlements?

The text explains that (a) Alluvial plains of Ganga (b) Arid and semi-arid regions of Rajasthan (c) Lower valleys of Himalayas (d) Forests and hills in north-east 2.

The text explains that (ii) What are the main factors for the location of villages in desert regions?

The text explains that (i) Discuss the features of different types of rural settlements.

The text explains that what are the factors responsible for the settlement patterns in different physical environments?

The text explains that (ii) Can one imagine the presence of only one-function town?

Source: NCERT Class 12 India People and Economy, Chapter 2: Migration

prelims_traps: Do not treat HUMAN and SETTLEMENTS as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Migration; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Migration through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use HUMAN and SETTLEMENTS to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
